title: Spinning Across Scales
text:
The question was never about cup size. It was about what her body was for.

I often say that without tennis, I might never have finished my Ph.D.
During my years at the University of Florida, tennis gave me much more than exercise. It gave me a community when I had none. It became my way of surviving the loneliness and pressure of graduate school. Many of those friends remain among my closest friends today.
Perhaps that is why I have always paid attention to stories from the tennis world.
Not only to championships.
But to the lives behind them.
Among all the players I have admired, one story has stayed with me for years.
It was not about winning a Grand Slam.
It was not about extraordinary talent.
It was about a Romanian player who made an unusual decision.
She underwent breast reduction surgery.
Not because of illness.
Not because of appearance.
But because she believed her body was preventing her from becoming the player she wanted to be.
After the first surgery, her ranking climbed from outside the Top 100 into the Top 100.
A year later, she chose a second reduction.
Soon she entered the Top 20.
Eventually, she became World No. 1.
I have no idea whether surgery alone changed her career.
That isn’t the point.
The story stayed with me because it quietly asked a question that reaches far beyond tennis.
What is your body for?
Growing up, I rarely heard that question.
Instead, I heard another one.
How should a woman look?
For a long time, I thought the answer was obvious.
Slimmer.
Fairer.
More feminine.
More attractive.
The body existed, consciously or unconsciously, to satisfy someone else’s expectations.
Years later, I realized that elite sport asks an entirely different question.
Not:

How attractive can my body become?

But:

What kind of body allows me to become the person I want to be?

Those are two completely different optimization problems.
One optimizes appearance.
The other optimizes purpose.
For a brief moment, I thought the conversation around women’s bodies had begun to change.
Strength became fashionable.
Women were encouraged to lift weights instead of simply losing weight.
I welcomed it.
Then, almost without anyone noticing, “strength” quietly became another aesthetic standard.
Now the ideal woman was expected to have visible abs.
The body had changed.
The gaze had not.
People were still looking at women.
They were simply looking for different things.
Whenever I hear people celebrating visible strength, I cannot help smiling.
I often joke with my friends:

A scientist’s cerebral cortex is a scientist’s six-pack.

Not every kind of strength can be photographed.
Some strength is built by asking difficult questions for years.
Some by staying curious when certainty is easier.
Some by changing your mind when the evidence changes.
Those muscles are invisible.
Yet they shape the world far more than the visible ones.
Perhaps this is why that tennis story has never really been about tennis.
Nor has it been about surgery.
It has always been about purpose.
Every society teaches us what to optimize.
Beauty.
Youth.
Productivity.
Strength.
Achievement.
Very few ask a more fundamental question:

What kind of life are you trying to build?

Because once you know the answer,
your body,
your career,
your relationships,
and even your ambitions
begin to reorganize themselves around it.
Perhaps freedom is not the ability to become anything we want.
Perhaps freedom begins when we are finally allowed to choose what we are optimizing for.

title: The Cost After the Numbers
text:
I thought I was calculating ecological damage. I didn’t realize I was also calculating the weight of scientific responsibility.

Seven and a half hours.
Twenty-six kilometers.
Across mountains scarred by illegal mining.
When I returned from the field, I thought the most difficult part of the investigation was behind me.
I was wrong.
The hardest part began after I submitted the report.

The landscape itself carried the memory of a development philosophy once proudly summarized by a local saying:

“Better to choke than to starve.”

Economic growth at any cost.
Factories before forests.
Production before consequences.
Walking those mountains, I wasn’t simply measuring ecological damage.
I was walking through the physical remains of an idea.
Every abandoned road.
Every excavated hillside.
Every dry stream.
Asked the same question.
What happens when today’s survival becomes tomorrow’s debt?

Weeks later, after the field survey, calculations, and endless revisions, I finally submitted the ecological damage assessment report.
Only then did I fully understand what I had actually written.
It was no longer just a scientific report.
It was evidence.
The number I calculated—the monetary value of ecological damage—would become part of a courtroom decision.
It might determine another person’s sentence.
At that moment, I felt a responsibility I had never encountered during six years of scientific training.
I had learned how to design research.
How to collect data.
How to quantify uncertainty.
No one had taught me what it meant when science entered the courtroom.

The burden was not only legal.
It was scientific.
Ecological damage assessment was still a young discipline.
The methods were evolving.
Many assumptions remained uncertain.
Yet the legal system required something science rarely promises:
A precise number.
A final answer.
As if uncertainty itself could be reduced to a single figure.
For the first time in my career, I wondered whether my report deserved the authority it had been given.

Not long afterward, I received notice that I might be asked to testify in court.
I refused.
Not because I doubted my work.
But because I realized the report no longer belonged entirely to science.
It had entered another world.
A world where evidence, law, politics, and power intersected in ways I could neither control nor fully understand.
I also began to worry that the report could become something I had never intended it to be.
A weapon.
Not only against environmental destruction.
But perhaps within political struggles hidden far beyond my field investigation.
Whether that fear was justified almost became secondary.
The realization itself changed me.

That investigation marked the first time I understood that scientific neutrality is not something a scientist simply possesses.
It is something we struggle to protect.
Sometimes successfully.
Sometimes not.
Science may begin with curiosity.
But once it leaves our hands, it acquires consequences that no statistical model can predict.

I still believe ecological damage should be investigated.
I still believe science belongs in public decision-making.
But since that investigation, I have carried a different question into every project.
It is no longer simply:

“Is my analysis scientifically rigorous?”

It is also:

“Am I prepared for what happens after the science leaves my hands?”

Perhaps the hardest part of becoming a scientist is not learning how to calculate uncertainty.
It is learning how to live with responsibility.

title: Journey Template
text:
Location · Years


“A short reflection on how this place changed the way I think.”

title: Journey Template
text:
The Story

Before I Arrived
Use this space for a quiet opening that situates the place and the state of mind you brought with you. Keep it reflective and grounded rather than descriptive for its own sake.


What I Observed
Describe the patterns, textures, and rhythms of the place. Focus on what became legible only once you were there.


What Changed
Explain the shift in perception. This is where the place becomes more than a setting and begins to reshape how you think.


What Stayed With Me
Close with a brief reflection on the lasting idea, question, or method that remained with you after leaving.

title: Journey Template
text:
Thinking Across Scales
This is the signature section. Summarize how this place changed your understanding of systems, scale, feedback, or complexity. Keep it clear, concise, and slightly more distilled than the main narrative.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
这个故事的背景是，我准备了一年的写了20页的proposal，好不容易在内部一个小型交流会，小范围内上讨论，我声嘶力竭讲了半小时，但是immediate reporting line 却毫无动容，可以说是表情毫无起伏，如同我一年前提出这个想法的雏形的时候。那是一个周五下午的阔部门内部会议，讲完之后我沿着american river trail走了1个小时，想这到底是为什么，除了政府的官僚之外，最深层次的原因这个proposal没被接受。我这个是要infrastructure innovation, 尺度很大。
做 STREAM proposal 的时候，我一直很困惑：为什么我看到整个工作流的瓶颈，Supervisor 看不到；Branch Manager 也看不到；甚至很多同事每天都在重复低效率的工作，却没有人提出改变？
后来我意识到，这未必是能力的问题，而是尺度（Scale）的问题。
我关注的是 Root Question（根问题）：
为什么整个系统会这样运作？
而他们每天必须回答的是：
今天有没有完成任务？ 预算有没有超支？ 有没有按时交付？ 人员有没有安排好？
这些问题都是真实的，也都很重要。
只是，它们发生在不同的尺度。
Supervisor 的尺度，是一个 Team。
Branch Manager 的尺度，是一个 Branch。
Division Chief 的尺度，是整个 Division。
而我当时试图解决的，却是：
整个 Workflow 为什么会变成今天这个样子？
这已经不是管理一个流程，而是在重新设计一个系统。
所以，我后来不再问：
为什么他们看不到？
而开始问：
他们每天被迫看什么？
一个人的职位，不仅决定了他的权力，也决定了他的视野。
职责，本身就是一种尺度。
这也是为什么，我后来修改了 STREAM proposal。
我不再一开始讨论：
Cloud。
Automation。
Architecture。
而是先做一个很小的 Proof of Concept。
不是因为技术需要。
而是因为：
我要把一个系统尺度的问题，翻译成他们能够观察到的尺度。
我越来越觉得：
创新，并不是提出别人没有想到的答案。
创新首先是一种：
跨尺度翻译（Translation Across Scales）。
把长期问题翻译成今天的决策。
把系统问题翻译成一个人的工作。
把未来十年的收益，翻译成下个月可以看到的成果。
所以，Thinking Across Scales，并不仅仅是生态学的方法。
它也是一种沟通的方法。
一种领导力。
甚至是一种理解他人的方式。

title: Thinking Across Scales

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Working title
Ecological Accounting: Thinking about Ecological Compensation and Natural Resource Damage Assessment as Two Temporal Forms of the Same Theory. Core idea
Ecological payment
↓
Pay before degradation.
Ecological damage assessment
↓
Pay after degradation.
Both are attempts to assign value to ecosystem services across time. Status
🌱 Seed
Natural resource damage assessment and ecological compensation are not separate policies, but two complementary ways of assigning value to ecosystems across time.
Evidence
Guizhou Hainan …
Questions
How did this framework evolve? How is it interpreted differently in China and elsewhere?

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Responsibility Determines Scale Why We See Different Problems
An unfinished note on leadership, communication, and innovation.
On a Friday afternoon, after spending nearly a year developing a proposal for infrastructure innovation, I finally had thirty minutes to present it.
It wasn’t a large audience—just an internal meeting with colleagues and managers from different branches.
I spoke with everything I had. I explained the workflow bottlenecks, the architecture, the pilot, and the long-term vision.
When I finished, I looked around the room.
Nothing.
No excitement.
No resistance.
No curiosity.
Only the same calm expressions I had seen a year earlier when I first shared the idea.
After the meeting, I drove to the American River Parkway and walked for nearly an hour.
I wasn’t asking why the proposal had failed.
I was asking a different question.
How could we look at the same proposal, yet see entirely different things?
It would have been easy to blame bureaucracy. Bureaucracy certainly mattered. But the longer I walked, the less convinced I became that it was the deepest explanation.
Instead, I began to suspect that the real difference was scale.
My proposal was asking a root question:
Why is the system designed this way in the first place?
The people around me were asking different questions:
Will this affect our current workflow? How much will it cost? Do we have the staff? Can we deliver this on time?
None of these questions were wrong.
They simply belonged to different scales.
Over time I realized something important.
People are not necessarily looking at the same problem and reaching different conclusions.
They are often looking at different scales of the same reality.
A supervisor sees a team.
A branch manager sees a branch.
An executive sees an organization.
Responsibility expands some horizons while inevitably narrowing others.
Responsibility determines scale.
Scale determines what becomes visible.
That realization changed the way I communicate.
I used to believe that when people didn’t understand an idea, I simply needed to provide more context.
Now I think the challenge is different.
Context explains.
Scale aligns.
Before discussing solutions, I first try to align the scale from which we are observing the problem.
Innovation is rarely rejected because people lack intelligence. More often, it is because they are looking from different scales of responsibility. Before changing minds, we must first align scales.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The Five Elements of Leadership — Reflections on Trust and Inspire
After reading Trust and Inspire, I found myself reflecting on the kind of leadership it describes through the lens of the Five Elements in Chinese philosophy — Metal, Water, Fire, Earth, and Wood. These elements represent not just the physical world but also human traits and relationships. Here’s how I see them shaping an inspired leader:
Metal: Leadership, like metal, is forged through pressure and experience. A strong leader is sharp in insight and resilient under pressure. Just as metal can cut through confusion, a seasoned leader makes clear decisions and stays steady in times of uncertainty.
Water: Water is gentle yet powerful. An inspiring leader can quietly nurture growth, like morning dew on young leaves. But when needed, they can also rally others and make waves of meaningful change — not through force, but through the collective momentum of trust and alignment. They know when to flow and when to gather strength.
Fire: Fire brings warmth and light. A great leader burns with passion — not just ambition, but a deep sense of purpose that energizes others. In dark or difficult times, they are the torchbearers who inspire courage and clarity.
Earth: Earth is stability and support. True leadership means creating a fertile space where people feel safe to grow, explore, and take risks. Like rich soil, the leader grounds the team in values and offers space for innovation to take root and thrive.
Wood: Wood represents growth and vision. A trusted and inspiring leader is like a tall tree — deeply rooted in trust, reaching upward with purpose. They branch out, connecting people and ideas, and provide shelter, strength, and direction for those around them.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Y博士巡护祖国生态系列之内蒙古包头站–非法采矿生态破坏鉴定评估。7个半小时26公里的山路，走的不是辛苦，是心酸。曾经当地领导的发展观是“宁愿呛死也不愿饿死”，不计代价不顾后果的上项目拼指标换来的是什么？死路一条？此路不通
后来把损害评估报告写完交上去之后，我才暗暗发觉，首先，这份报告是环境法庭用来判案的依据，我最后这个损失额度是用来定罪的，就是说我的调查决定了某个人的命运。我觉得担子很重，而且评估系统没有建立，评价方法很不完善，这一纸报告对不起我六年来的严苛科学训练。我勉强交了报告，但是拒绝出庭（后来一度我被通知，要准备出庭作证）。再有，我这个调查被当作枪，借刀杀人，这项损害评估很可能被卷入到当地的政治斗争。感觉我的科学良心被审判了，被判无期。

title: 鹅掌楸之殇
text:
5/17/2017 尽管穿越了第四纪冰期的严寒，却无法逃脱挖掘机的铁爪。国家二级保护植物、古老的木兰科被子植物鹅掌楸Chinese tulip tree（别名：马褂木）原生天然林在贵州省某地被铲平，倒在风力发电的铺路石下！同为国家二级保护植物的凹叶厚朴同样深陷困境，前途未知！是要保路还是要护树？不是两难问题，是表面问题。可再生能源的开发是可持续发展？未必。深藏幕后的核心冲突是经济发展和生态保护？在我看来也不是。
我作为一个调查人员和某国营风力发电公司的坐在同一辆车上，他们很自豪，进山里的路就是他们公司修起来的。我坐在这样的车里，四个人，司机，负责接待的办事员，首先觉得这样的被告和裁判没有独立，作为调查人员不能单独调查，去任何地方都要他们带路让我失去了行动自由，也就意味着我是不可能有独立的思考的。这里设计国家制度设计的司法独立，已经不能在这里这个简单的故事里讨论了。作为tech geek， 我最好奇的是选址，为什么要在贵州这个山区，这么偏僻的地方建风电？？这个问题我最费解，怎么看都不是合适的地方。首先就是没有风！风力能达到最低选址要求吗？我就问这个办事人员，风电的费用啊，规模啊，他告诉我的是，现在的建风电场主要是国家补贴！我一听就恼火，我就知道，一方面是作为科学家的hypothsis testing 这个地方达不到风力等级,得到证实。另一方吗就是因为，这个熟悉的故事，国家意志凌驾于生态、民生。因为第一次近距离看风电，我有巨物恐惧症啊，看到工人们在施工拼装的时候，我还是很震惊，每到下车活动手脚的时候都跟民工师傅们交流，我从来不觉得他们是‘下等公民’，而相反，他们是unsung hero! 当然这个解读肯定是美利坚的，在国内的叙事是奉献。我给他们拍照，我想把他们在国家第一线，了无人烟的工地热火朝天的工作场景记录下来，我说要发给他，他很高兴，说要发给家里人。我想为什么问他们自己不拍照吗？他们也不是没有手机。转头一想，我自己有了答案，而是工作已经耗尽了所有，眼前只有材米油盐，而不是诗和远方。更加不会把自己的工作上升到无名英雄而自豪吧。

title: Thinking Across Scales is grounded in several organizing principles:
text:
Thinking Across Scales is grounded in several organizing principles:
• Feedback • Scale • Recursive learning • Resilience • Regenerative systems • Emergence • Modularity

title: Journey Index Page Specification
text:
Design the index page for the Journeys section of my Quarto website.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is an atlas of places that fundamentally transformed how I think.
The page should communicate curiosity, reflection, exploration, and intellectual growth.
This page serves as the primary navigation page for all Journey articles.

title: About
text:
I am an environmental scientist, writer, and systems thinker.
My work explores how ecological thinking can illuminate not only natural systems, but also organizations, cultures, and the human mind.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The template organizes the page, but never dictates the narrative.
The English version is not a translation of the Chinese. It is another telling of the same journey.
Every Journey should have one sentence that cannot be forgotten.

title: World Map Component
text:
Create a reusable SVG world map component for a Quarto website.
The map should become the primary navigation interface for the Journeys section.
It should be lightweight.
No JavaScript.
Use inline SVG.
Design Style
Minimal
Editorial
Muted earth tones
Thin coastlines
Lots of whitespace
Do not use Google Maps.
Do not use Leaflet.
Do not use Mapbox.
Locations
Everglades
Yellowstone
Yunnan
Tibet
Maine
Sacramento
Each location should appear as a circular marker.
Each marker should link to its corresponding Journey page.
The SVG should scale responsively.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
You are an expert Quarto website developer, UX designer, and frontend engineer.
Your task is to create a production-ready landing page (index.qmd) for my personal website.
Do not explain your reasoning.
Generate clean, maintainable Quarto Markdown with Bootstrap 5.
Do not use JavaScript.
Do not use external frameworks.
Only generate code.



PROJECT



Website Title:
Thinking Across Scales
Website Theme:
A digital garden documenting how landscape ecology evolved into a broader way of understanding systems, feedback, patterns, dynamics, and scale across science, technology, organizations, and everyday life.
The design should feel like:
• Apple • Notion • Linear • Stripe • Modern academic website
Keywords:
minimal elegant scientific storytelling lots of whitespace responsive timeless



LAYOUT



Create the following sections.
==================================================== 1. HERO ====================================================
Large centered title:
Thinking Across Scales
Subtitle:
How studying landscapes became a way of understanding the world.
Use a full-width hero image.
For now use a placeholder image.
Later I will replace it with my own Everglades photograph.
The hero should occupy about 60–70% of the first screen.
Large typography.
Minimal overlay.
==================================================== 2. INTRODUCTION ====================================================
Below the hero.
Use approximately 120 words.
Text:
I was trained as a landscape ecologist, where the most important lesson was not about landscapes themselves, but about learning to think across scales. Over time, that perspective expanded beyond ecology into computation, organizations, design, and everyday life. This website is an evolving record of that journey.
Centered with generous margins.
==================================================== 3. FEATURE CARDS ====================================================
Create four equal Bootstrap cards.
Card 1
Title
Thinking Across Scales
Description
Ideas about feedback, hierarchy, dynamics, and systems.

Card 2
Title
Journeys
Description
Places that transformed how I think.

Card 3
Title
Computational Thinking
Description
Modern scientific workflows and digital craftsmanship.

Card 4
Title
Writing
Description
Essays, notes, and ongoing reflections.

Each card should contain:
Bootstrap Icon
Title
Description
Simple “Explore →” link
Hover effect using CSS only.
==================================================== 4. FEATURED CONTENT ====================================================
Create two side-by-side highlight panels.
Left
Featured Essay
Title:
Why Scale Matters
Placeholder image
Short description
Button:
Read More →

Right
Latest Journey
Title:
Everglades
Placeholder image
Short description
Button:
View Journey →
==================================================== 5. QUOTE ====================================================
Create a full-width elegant quote section.
Quote:
“I am not interested in whether two systems look alike.
I am interested in whether they are organized by the same principles.”
Large serif typography.
Centered.
Subtle background color.
==================================================== 6. FOOTER ====================================================
Simple footer.
Left
Thinking Across Scales
Center
Built with Quarto
Right
Hosted on GitHub



STYLE



Use Bootstrap 5.
Responsive on desktop and mobile.
Lots of whitespace.
Avoid heavy borders.
Use subtle shadows.
Rounded corners.
Professional typography.
No flashy animations.
No JavaScript.



OUTPUT



Generate:

index.qmd
assets/css/custom.css
Any required YAML.

Use relative paths.
The code should be ready to render immediately in Quarto.
Return only code.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Create a reusable Quarto page template for a Journey.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is a story about how a place transformed the way I think.
The design should feel calm, spacious, reflective, and editorial.
Use the same visual language as the Landing Page.

Page Structure

Hero Section

Large immersive landscape image
Journey title
Location
Years
One-sentence reflection
Example:
Everglades
Florida, USA
2008–2012
“The landscape where I first learned to think across scales.”


At a Glance

Display as four elegant cards
Location
Years
Themes
Related Research


The Story

Long-form narrative.
Use large readable typography.
Break into sections.
Suggested headings:
Before I Arrived
What I Observed
What Changed
What Stayed With Me


Gallery

Responsive image gallery.
Large images with short captions.


Thinking Across Scales

This is the signature section.
Summarize how this place changed my understanding of systems, scale, feedback, or complexity.
This section should be visually distinguished from the main story.


Related Essays

Display 3 linked essays as cards.

Design Principles
Minimal
Editorial
Elegant
Lots of whitespace
No unnecessary decorations
Responsive
Use Bootstrap Grid
Do not invent placeholder content beyond demonstrating layout.
Focus on creating a reusable template rather than a finished article.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The Mapbox map is working correctly.
Please improve the implementation without changing the overall architecture.
Current implementation
However, several improvements are needed.

title: Thinking Across Scales
section: 1. Automatically fit the map to all locations
text:
1. Automatically fit the map to all locations
Remove the fixed
center: [...]
zoom: ...
Instead, after all markers are created:

Create a mapboxgl.LngLatBounds object.
Extend the bounds with every location.
Call map.fitBounds(bounds).

Use approximately
padding: 80
maxZoom: 2.8
This ensures the map automatically adjusts whenever new places are added.
Do not hard-code the map center.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
2. Improve the default world view
The initial map should display North America and East Asia together.
The current view is centered too far over the Indian Ocean.
The map should feel like a global atlas.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
3. Improve marker styling
Replace the default black markers with a cleaner editorial style.
Use small circular markers.
White fill.
Dark border.
Subtle hover animation.
Markers should feel elegant rather than like Google Maps.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
4. Improve popups
Current popup only displays title and summary.
Instead display
Journey title
Region
Summary
A small “Read Journey →” link
Use clean typography.
Rounded popup.
Maximum width around 260px.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
5. Better map interaction
Disable
rotation
pitch
double-click zoom
Enable
mouse wheel zoom
drag
touch navigation
The interaction should feel calm.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
6. Remove unnecessary UI
Hide
Mapbox attribution (if legally permitted)
Compass
Rotation controls
Keep the interface minimal.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
7. Improve code organization
Split the implementation into reusable functions.
Example
initializeMap()
loadPlaces()
createMarkers()
fitMapToPlaces()
The code should become modular and easier to maintain.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
8. Future compatibility
Keep the implementation compatible with future additions such as
Timeline
Journey filtering
Marker categories
Animated routes
Do not implement these features now.
Simply organize the code so future expansion is straightforward.

Do not redesign the page.
Only improve the Mapbox component.
The goal is to create a reusable World Map component for the entire website.

title: Journey Pipeline
text:
Transform raw travel materials into a published Journey page while preserving the author’s voice. Preserve the author’s narrative voice. Never replace a vivid scene with a structural heading. Structure should guide the writing, not become visible to the reader.





garden/capture/.md
Photos (optional)





Step 1
Read the capture file.
Do not rewrite immediately.
Identify

timeline
important scenes
turning points
recurring themes


Step 2
Produce a curated Journey Note.
Requirements

preserve my voice
remove repetition
do not invent facts
keep chronology
highlight insights

Save as
garden/notes/.md

Step 3
Review manually.
Add reflections that only I can write.

Step 4
Generate
journeys/.qmd
using
journeys/journey-template.qmd

Step 5
Link images.
Export only web-sized images.

Output
A published Journey page.

title: 青海杜鹃的白色恐怖
text:
如果不是满山盛开的白色青海杜鹃，积雪祁连山山顶下开阔的草原，清冽的高海拔空气，我可能会随着被挖坑河滩和被掘空的山体而心中空空。上边的政策，下边的烟头。
驱车来到河边，我站在非法采石堆成的小山包那里测量距离，这样回去可以跟上司汇报，估算项目的费用。来接待我的办事员站在不远处，小个头， 干瘦，肤色也是典型的高原色，应该是少数名族小伙，本地口音很重。手里的烟头自打我测量起就没有停过，一根儿接着一根儿。这可是海拔4000多的高原啦，肺能受得了吗？看着他碾碎在河滩边的一地烟头，我不禁感概万千：近年来，政府的红头文件三天两头发，前有生态红线，后有生态损害赔偿，中间还有绿色GDP，每一个文件都好似他手里的那根浸满尼古丁的烟，兴奋一天，结果一溜烟儿，后果是一辈子，老百姓的一生。
河边的石矿看完，他领着我往山上走去。这是个河谷，两边的山顶还有积雪，走上山顶可以俯瞰整个河谷。山坡背阴面是地毯版的青海杜鹃，在这个季节盛开，乳白色的大花心中只有对风景的向往，但真正来到半山腰，另一个非法采石的地点，看着一整个巨大的山体被削去一刀，我的心也被割去了一块。

objectID: garden/capture/qinghai-wechat.html#y博士巡护祖国生态之青海站

title: 青海杜鹃的白色恐怖
text:
如果不是满山盛开的白色青海杜鹃，积雪祁连山山顶下开阔的草原，清冽的高海拔空气，我可能会随着被挖坑河滩和被掘空的山体而心中空空。上边的政策，下边的烟头。
驱车来到河边，我站在非法采石堆成的小山包那里测量距离，这样回去可以跟上司汇报，估算项目的费用。来接待我的办事员站在不远处，小个头， 干瘦，肤色也是典型的高原色，应该是少数名族小伙，本地口音很重。手里的烟头自打我测量起就没有停过，一根儿接着一根儿。这可是海拔4000多的高原啦，肺能受得了吗？看着他碾碎在河滩边的一地烟头，我不禁感概万千：近年来，政府的红头文件三天两头发，前有生态红线，后有生态损害赔偿，中间还有绿色GDP，每一个文件都好似他手里的那根浸满尼古丁的烟，兴奋一天，结果一溜烟儿，后果是一辈子，老百姓的一生。
河边的石矿看完，他领着我往山上走去。这是个河谷，两边的山顶还有积雪，走上山顶可以俯瞰整个河谷。山坡背阴面是地毯版的青海杜鹃，在这个季节盛开，乳白色的大花心中只有对风景的向往，但真正来到半山腰，另一个非法采石的地点，看着一整个巨大的山体被削去一刀，我的心也被割去了一块。

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The Fall of a Tulip Tree
Guizhou, China · May 2017
It had survived the ice ages of the Quaternary, only to fall beneath the steel teeth of an excavator.
A stand of wild Chinese tulip trees (Liriodendron chinense), an ancient magnolia lineage and a nationally protected species in China, had been cleared to make way for a road leading to a wind farm. Nearby, another protected species, Magnolia officinalis var. biloba, faced an uncertain future.
At first glance, the question seemed obvious: should we save the road or save the trees?
But standing there, I realized that was the wrong question.
Was renewable energy always sustainable? Not necessarily.
Was this simply a conflict between economic development and environmental protection? I no longer believed that either.
Something deeper was unfolding beneath the surface.
I arrived at the site in a vehicle provided by the state-owned wind power company itself.
They were proud of the road we were driving on—it had been built by their company.
As an investigator, however, I could not ignore the irony.
The people leading the investigation were also the people being investigated.
Every destination depended on their guidance. Every conversation happened on their terms.
Long before I reached the forest, I had already begun wondering whether independent observation was even possible.
During every break, I wandered over to talk with the construction workers.
I never saw them as “ordinary laborers.”
If anything, they were the anonymous builders carrying the weight of the country’s ambitions on their shoulders.
I photographed them as they assembled turbines against the mountains.
One worker smiled when I promised to send him the photos so he could show his family.
For a moment, I wondered why he had never taken these pictures himself.
Then the answer came quietly.
By the end of the day, work had already consumed everything.
Survival leaves little room for poetry.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
White Terror of the Qinghai Rhododendrons
Qinghai Province · Summer 2017
Had it not been for the white rhododendrons blanketing the mountainside, the snow lingering on the peaks of the Qilian Range, and the crystalline air of four thousand meters, I might have left with the same emptiness carved into the riverbanks and hollowed out of the mountains themselves.
Policies descended from above.
Cigarette butts accumulated below.
I walked across a mound of illegally excavated gravel, measuring distances that would later become estimates in a damage assessment report.
A local official waited nearby.
Small in stature, weathered by the plateau, and speaking with a thick local accent, he never stopped smoking.
One cigarette after another.
We were standing more than four thousand meters above sea level.
I could not help wondering how his lungs endured what the altitude had already made scarce.
In recent years, environmental policies had arrived one after another.
Ecological redlines.
Ecological compensation.
Green GDP.
Each new directive burned brightly for a day, much like the cigarette between his fingers.
Then the smoke drifted away.
The consequences, however, remained for a lifetime. We climbed higher into the valley.
Snow still rested on the ridges above us.
The shaded slopes were covered by carpets of Qinghai rhododendrons in full bloom.
Their pale blossoms seemed to exist for beauty alone.
Then, around the bend, another quarry appeared.
An entire mountainside had been sliced away.
Standing there, I felt as though something had been cut from me as well.

title: Spinning Across Scales
text:
The question was never about cup size. It was about what her body was for.

I often say that without tennis, I might never have finished my Ph.D.
During my years at the University of Florida, tennis gave me much more than exercise. It gave me a community when I had none. It became my way of surviving the loneliness and pressure of graduate school. Many of those friends remain among my closest friends today.
Perhaps that is why I have always paid attention to stories from the tennis world.
Not only to championships.
But to the lives behind them.
Among all the players I have admired, one story has stayed with me for years.
It was not about winning a Grand Slam.
It was not about extraordinary talent.
It was about a Romanian player who made an unusual decision.
She underwent breast reduction surgery.
Not because of illness.
Not because of appearance.
But because she believed her body was preventing her from becoming the player she wanted to be.
After the first surgery, her ranking climbed from outside the Top 100 into the Top 100.
A year later, she chose a second reduction.
Soon she entered the Top 20.
Eventually, she became World No. 1.
I have no idea whether surgery alone changed her career.
That isn’t the point.
The story stayed with me because it quietly asked a question that reaches far beyond tennis.
What is your body for?
Growing up, I rarely heard that question.
Instead, I heard another one.
How should a woman look?
For a long time, I thought the answer was obvious.
Slimmer.
Fairer.
More feminine.
More attractive.
The body existed, consciously or unconsciously, to satisfy someone else’s expectations.
Years later, I realized that elite sport asks an entirely different question.
Not:

How attractive can my body become?

But:

What kind of body allows me to become the person I want to be?

Those are two completely different optimization problems.
One optimizes appearance.
The other optimizes purpose.
For a brief moment, I thought the conversation around women’s bodies had begun to change.
Strength became fashionable.
Women were encouraged to lift weights instead of simply losing weight.
I welcomed it.
Then, almost without anyone noticing, “strength” quietly became another aesthetic standard.
Now the ideal woman was expected to have visible abs.
The body had changed.
The gaze had not.
People were still looking at women.
They were simply looking for different things.
Whenever I hear people celebrating visible strength, I cannot help smiling.
I often joke with my friends:

A scientist’s cerebral cortex is a scientist’s six-pack.

Not every kind of strength can be photographed.
Some strength is built by asking difficult questions for years.
Some by staying curious when certainty is easier.
Some by changing your mind when the evidence changes.
Those muscles are invisible.
Yet they shape the world far more than the visible ones.
Perhaps this is why that tennis story has never really been about tennis.
Nor has it been about surgery.
It has always been about purpose.
Every society teaches us what to optimize.
Beauty.
Youth.
Productivity.
Strength.
Achievement.
Very few ask a more fundamental question:

What kind of life are you trying to build?

Because once you know the answer,
your body,
your career,
your relationships,
and even your ambitions
begin to reorganize themselves around it.
Perhaps freedom is not the ability to become anything we want.
Perhaps freedom begins when we are finally allowed to choose what we are optimizing for.

title: Ecology of Ideas
text:
Ecology of Ideas
Status: 🌱 Seed
One sentence
Explore how ecological ideas travel across cultures, generations, and political systems through conversations with overseas Chinese ecologists.
Questions

Why did they become ecologists?
How did China shape their thinking?
How did the U.S. change their thinking?
Which ecological concepts have been reinterpreted in China?
How has ecology become part of national narratives?

Possible outputs

Documentary series
Podcast
Essays
Interactive map
Interview archive

Possible sponsors

ESA
Universities
Foundations

Related
Thinking Across Scales Journey The Ecology of the Mind

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
我热衷网球，就如同我热爱研究。我曾经说过，如果我没有打网球，可能我的博士无法毕业。在佛罗里达大学念博士期间，网球给了我一个朋友圈，现在和他们都保持联系的朋友；一种面对高压的学术压力的减压方式。然而这么多网球球员的故事里，除了我的网球偶像李娜的故事，我印象最深的是罗马尼亚女球手，我在国内的时候就听过，一个网球选手为了取得更好的网球成绩去做了缩胸手术, 先是缩到了c cup, 结果成绩从一百名开外，提升到了百名之内；然后一年之后再次手术，结果成绩提升到了前20，之后更是一路飞升，来到世界第一。这个故事特别讲给女性，东亚女性。因为她们最流行的手术是隆胸…你要追求的是什么？
曾经有一段时间，国内的女性运动变成欣赏有力量的女性，被扭曲成，有八块腹肌的女性。 我对比表示很不理解：我常说科学家的大脑皮层褶皱，就是科学家的八块腹肌。
东亚女性面对的特殊困境，取悦异性：封建、父权社会。这个是西方社会没有的，特别是封建。这个题目，我虽然写的是spining across scales, 但是副标题，我想玩梗，从F到C到B，她最终成为A，这个对应者学术圈我们作业对学术的评分，从F代表fail， 到C…

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Thinking Across Scales
How studying landscapes became a way of understanding the world.




I was trained as a landscape ecologist, where the most important lesson was not about landscapes themselves, but about learning to think across scales. Over time, that perspective expanded beyond ecology into computation, organizations, design, and everyday life. This website is an evolving record of that journey.







⟲


Thinking Across Scales
Ideas about feedback, hierarchy, dynamics, and systems.
Explore →






📍


Journeys
Places that transformed how I think.
Explore →






⌘


Computational Thinking
Modern scientific workflows and digital craftsmanship.
Explore →






✍️


Writing
Essays, notes, and ongoing reflections.
Explore →













Featured Essay

Why Scale Matters
An exploration of scale as a guiding principle for science, design, and systems thinking.
Read More →










Latest Journey

Everglades
A field story about water, fire, networks, and the scales where landscape processes become visible.
View Journey →








“I am not interested in whether two systems look alike. I am interested in whether they are organized by the same principles.”

Hosted on GitHub

text:
The Fall of a Tulip Tree
Guizhou, China · May 2017
It had survived the ice ages of the Quaternary, only to fall beneath the steel teeth of an excavator.
A stand of wild Chinese tulip trees (Liriodendron chinense), an ancient magnolia lineage and a nationally protected species in China, had been cleared to make way for a road leading to a wind farm. Nearby, another protected species, Magnolia officinalis var. biloba, faced an uncertain future.
At first glance, the question seemed obvious: should we save the road or save the trees?
But standing there, I realized that was the wrong question.

Was this simply a conflict between economic development and environmental protection? I no longer believed that either.

I arrived at the site in a vehicle provided by the state-owned wind power company itself.
They were proud of the road we were driving on—it had been built by their company.
As an investigator, however, I could not ignore the irony.
The people leading the investigation were also the people being investigated.
Every destination depended on their guidance. Every conversation happened on their terms.
Long before I reached the forest, I had already begun wondering whether independent observation was even possible.
During every break, I wandered over to talk with the construction workers.
I never saw them as “ordinary laborers.”
If anything, they were the anonymous builders carrying the weight of the country’s ambitions on their shoulders.
I photographed them as they assembled turbines against the mountains.
One worker smiled when I promised to send him the photos so he could show his family.
For a moment, I wondered why he had never taken these pictures himself.
Then the answer came quietly.
By the end of the day, work had already consumed everything.
Survival leaves little room for poetry.

text:

Some places taught me ecology. Some taught me systems. Some taught me computation. Some revealed entirely new ways of seeing. Together they became the foundation of Thinking Across Scales.

title: Responsibility Determines Scale
text:
Responsibility Determines Scale Why We See Different Problems
An unfinished note on leadership, communication, and innovation.
On a Friday afternoon, after spending nearly a year developing a proposal for infrastructure innovation, I finally had thirty minutes to present it.
It wasn’t a large audience—just an internal meeting with colleagues and managers from different branches.
I spoke with everything I had. I explained the workflow bottlenecks, the architecture, the pilot, and the long-term vision.
When I finished, I looked around the room.
Nothing.
No excitement.
No resistance.
No curiosity.
Only the same calm expressions I had seen a year earlier when I first shared the idea.
After the meeting, I drove to the American River Parkway and walked for nearly an hour.
I wasn’t asking why the proposal had failed.
I was asking a different question.
How could we look at the same proposal, yet see entirely different things?
It would have been easy to blame bureaucracy. Bureaucracy certainly mattered. But the longer I walked, the less convinced I became that it was the deepest explanation.
Instead, I began to suspect that the real difference was scale.
My proposal was asking a root question:
Why is the system designed this way in the first place?
The people around me were asking different questions:
Will this affect our current workflow? How much will it cost? Do we have the staff? Can we deliver this on time?
None of these questions were wrong.
They simply belonged to different scales.
Over time I realized something important.
People are not necessarily looking at the same problem and reaching different conclusions.
They are often looking at different scales of the same reality.
A supervisor sees a team.
A branch manager sees a branch.
An executive sees an organization.
Responsibility expands some horizons while inevitably narrowing others.
Responsibility determines scale.
Scale determines what becomes visible.
That realization changed the way I communicate.
I used to believe that when people didn’t understand an idea, I simply needed to provide more context.
Now I think the challenge is different.
Context explains.
Scale aligns.
Before discussing solutions, I first try to align the scale from which we are observing the problem.
Innovation is rarely rejected because people lack intelligence. More often, it is because they are looking from different scales of responsibility. Before changing minds, we must first align scales.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Thinking Across Scales

Every landscape is also a way of thinking.

We often explain problems at the scale of individuals, when their roots lie in larger systems. We debate policies at the national level, yet rarely ask how they are lived by a single person. Thinking Across Scales is the practice of moving continuously between these perspectives—between individuals and institutions, places and planets, moments and millennia. Ecological problems are fundamentally problems of scale. I have come to believe that life is, too.
I did not arrive at this idea through philosophy.
I arrived through fieldwork.
Years of ecological investigations across China’s forests, wetlands, grasslands, and mountains slowly revealed a recurring pattern.
The questions I carried into the field were rarely the questions I brought home.
A fallen tulip tree became a story about institutions. A cigarette butt became a story about policy. A wetland became a story about time.
The Ecology of the Mind is my attempt to continue these conversations.
It is not a travel blog.
It is not an academic website.
It is a living collection of journeys, essays, conversations, and ideas exploring how landscapes shape the ways we think—and how our ways of thinking, in turn, reshape the landscapes we inhabit.
This is where the journey begins.
